title: Beyond ‘blood gold’: understanding the spectrum of illicit gold conflict
gist: IISS proposes a three-tier spectrum for understanding illicit-gold conflict — from localised resource disputes, through mid-intensity conflict financing, to full 'gilded war economies' — arguing that whether a gold-producing country escalates along this spectrum depends chiefly on the character of its ruling regime (factionalised, militarised, territorially fragmented regimes are most at risk) rather than on gold production levels alone.

insights.claim: Gold's high value, portability and easy convertibility to cash and increasingly to cryptocurrencies make it an ideal transaction medium for organised criminal groups, non-state armed groups, and state or military actors seeking to evade anti-money-laundering rules or bypass legitimate supply chains.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: As crypto on-ramps make gold-to-cash conversion even easier, illicit gold's attractiveness as a war-financing instrument is likely to grow, a factor relevant to AML/sanctions-compliance and crypto-monitoring plays.
insights.locator: The high value, portability and easy convertibility of gold

insights.claim: Russia is reportedly recycling gold extracted from conflict-affected African states to help finance its war in Ukraine.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: Directly links African illicit-gold networks to a live, sanctioned state actor's war financing — relevant to sanctions-evasion tracking and gold-supply-chain due diligence for Russia-linked flows.
insights.locator: Russia is reportedly recycling gold extracted from conflict-affected African states

insights.claim: Illicit gold economies expand across borders, creating transnational networks that carry gold to major intermediary and destination markets, where it is gradually blended with legitimate gold supply.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: Blending with legitimate supply is the laundering mechanism that makes downstream gold buyers (refiners, jewellers, bullion markets) unwitting participants — a due-diligence and provenance-tracing risk.
insights.locator: These illicit gold economies can expand across borders

insights.claim: IISS frames illicit-gold conflicts on a spectrum with three tiers of intensity — localised resource conflicts, conflict financing within conventional civil wars, and full 'gilded war economies' — with conflicts becoming more organised and drawing in more powerful actors as they move along the spectrum.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: Gives investors and risk analysts a reusable classification tool to gauge how far a given gold-producing country's instability might escalate.
insights.locator: One way to make sense of conflicts linked to illicit gold

insights.claim: At the most extreme end of the spectrum, an actor's power can only be maintained through continued control of illicit gold, which becomes the foundation of a war economy binding together rival actors and their allies.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: Identifies the point of no return where gold ceases to be a funding source and becomes the structural glue of a conflict system, making such conflicts far harder to resolve through economic incentives alone.
insights.locator: At the most extreme end, the power of these actors

insights.claim: Whether localised gold-linked violence escalates depends heavily on whether powerful national or regional actors — regime-linked elites, neighbouring-country elites, or armed opposition groups — insert themselves; escalation is more likely when such actors are motivated by capturing revenue rather than restoring peace.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: A concrete early-warning signal: watch for elite or armed-group entry into local gold disputes as the trigger that converts contained violence into sustained conflict.
insights.locator: A key influence is the presence of more powerful national or regional actors

insights.claim: In northwestern Nigeria, where state authority has withered as armed groups multiplied, artisanal gold mining has become a focal point for predation by criminal 'bandit' groups.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: Flags northwestern Nigeria as a live case of state-authority collapse converging with gold predation — a specific country-risk data point for security and commodity-supply-chain assessments.
insights.locator: in northwestern Nigeria – where state authority has withered

insights.claim: In Papua New Guinea, inter-clan disputes over mining-area access have converged with discontent over land-use agreements with international mining firms, producing volatility and violence.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: A specific operational-risk case for international miners: land-use agreement disputes are a documented conflict trigger in PNG.
insights.locator: In Papua New Guinea, inter-clan disputes over access

insights.claim: Illicit gold typically sustains rather than primarily finances conventional civil wars, via direct/indirect production and trafficking by belligerents, taxation of gold at checkpoints, or extortion and kidnapping of miners and traders.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: Distinguishes gold as a sustaining rather than root cause of most civil wars — implies that cutting off gold revenue alone won't end these conflicts, only weaken belligerents' cash flow.
insights.locator: illicit gold can be used to sustain the warring parties

insights.claim: In the Congolese civil wars of the late 1990s and early 2000s, Ugandan and Rwandan military forces were closely involved in gold extraction and taxation to fund military operations and for personal financial gain, making illicit gold a significant driver of that conflict.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: A historical precedent showing neighbouring states' militaries directly profiting from conflict gold — relevant template for assessing current Rwanda-linked involvement in eastern DRC.
insights.locator: illicit gold played a more significant role in the Congolese civil wars

insights.claim: After the 2008 financial crisis, rising international gold prices drove insurgent and criminal groups in Colombia to escalate illicit gold trafficking in the early 2010s, with effects on post-conflict insecurity and ecosystems that persisted after the 2016 peace agreement.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: Links gold-price cycles directly to conflict-financing intensity — a macro variable (gold price) that risk models should treat as a leading indicator for gold-linked conflict in vulnerable producer states.
insights.locator: After the 2008 financial crisis, the (partial) financing

insights.claim: In the Central African Republic, artisanal miners and armed groups shifted from the internationally embargoed diamond trade (Kimberley Process, 2013) to illicit gold in the mid-2010s, with control over gold sites and transit routes becoming a driver of conflict between militias, insurgents and the military, the latter backed by the Wagner Group and the Rwandan army.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: Shows how commodity-specific sanctions/embargoes (Kimberley Process on diamonds) can simply displace conflict financing into an unregulated substitute commodity (gold) — a case study relevant to designing future resource-conflict interventions.
insights.locator: The conflict economy in the Central African Republic (CAR)

insights.claim: Illicit gold flows have become entangled in Myanmar's war economy since the country's return to civil war in 2021, with both military authorities and rebel movements capturing gold revenue.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: Identifies Myanmar as another active case where both sides of a civil war depend on gold revenue, relevant to sanctions and supply-chain screening on Myanmar-origin gold.
insights.locator: flows of illicit gold are becoming ensnared in Myanmar’s wider war economy

insights.claim: In eastern Democratic Republic of the Congo, competition over gold revenues has fragmented armed groups into smaller militias focused on controlling mines and trading routes, producing more localised and volatile conflict patterns.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: Gold-revenue competition is an active driver of militia fragmentation in eastern DRC — implies conflict there is likely to stay diffuse and hard to resolve via a single negotiated settlement.
insights.locator: In parts of the eastern Democratic Republic of the Congo

insights.claim: In the most extreme tier, unstable, transactionally-governed countries undergoing gold rushes see powerful elites and rival factions seize gold mines and block formalisation efforts, using gold revenue to consolidate power rather than fund economic recovery or institution-building.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: Predicts that post-conflict gold rushes in weakly-governed states will entrench predatory elites rather than aid reconstruction — a bearish signal for state-building and formal-mining investment in such contexts.
insights.locator: powerful elites and rival factions may seize control of gold mines

insights.claim: Outcomes diverge sharply among post-conflict gold producers with similar vulnerabilities: Côte d'Ivoire did not revert to large-scale conflict, Sudan descended into severe civil war, and Ethiopia's Tigray region saw a gold rush coincide with worsening internal and regional instability.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: Shows gold production alone doesn't determine relapse into war — an important caveat against naive resource-curse country screening.
insights.locator: Côte d’Ivoire did not revert to large-scale violent conflict

insights.claim: The nature of the ruling regime and political order explains divergent outcomes: militarised regimes that are internally factionalised and whose territorial control is fragmented are at particular risk of re-entering conflict as gold production rises.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: The single highest-value predictive variable in the piece: regime cohesion and territorial control, not gold volume alone, is the key risk screen for whether a gold boom triggers relapse into war.
insights.locator: The nature of the ruling regime and the structure

insights.claim: In a fully gilded war economy, belligerents and their allies become dependent on gold to sustain power, generate foreign exchange for imports and maintain partnerships with external states, while internal conflicts regionalise as insurgents move into new territory to control transport corridors.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: Explains the internal-to-regional escalation mechanism (control of transport corridors) that turns a domestic gold-financed war, like Sudan's or the Sahel's, into a cross-border security problem.
insights.locator: Belligerents and their allies become increasingly dependent

insights.claim: Gilded war economies are a 'potential time bomb' because intensive gold production cannot be sustained indefinitely; production decline may trigger further instability unless the war economy is rebalanced around more predictable revenue sources.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: Flags a forward risk: current gold-financed conflicts (Sudan, Sahel) may face a second destabilisation shock when production inevitably declines, a scenario worth pricing into long-horizon regional risk assessments.
insights.quote: These gilded war economies are also a potential time bomb.
insights.locator: These gilded war economies are also a potential time bomb

insights.claim: Three shared vulnerabilities apply across the whole spectrum of illicit-gold conflict: the rate at which illicit gold production is increasing, existing unresolved fault lines likely to be widened by competition for gold, and the presence of infrastructure to move gold discreetly to intermediary or destination markets.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: A practical three-factor screening checklist for assessing which gold-producing countries are most exposed to conflict risk going forward.
insights.locator: These include the scale at which illicit gold production
